home.title: Real ranking of Brazilian universities
home.subtitle: ENADE continuous score: no rounding, no marketing
home.onboarding: Pick a course to see the real university ranking. Select two institutions to compare scores.

compare.title: Comparison between institutions
compare.trap_body: {{ies_b}} has Band {{faixa}}, but its continuous score ({{score_b}}) is only {{diff_low}} points above the band's lower limit. {{ies_a}}, with {{score_a}}, is {{diff}} points ahead.
compare.evolution_single_year: Only one year of data available. The chart will populate as more years are processed.

about.title: About OpenEnade
about.openenade_body: OpenEnade exposes the ENADE continuous score, enabling fair comparisons between higher education institutions. While MEC only publishes the ENADE Concept (an integer from 1 to 5), we show the actual continuous score behind that concept.
about.what_is_enade_body: The National Student Performance Exam (ENADE) is administered by INEP/MEC to evaluate graduating students in undergraduate programs. The exam measures performance in relation to course content, competencies, and skills required for professional development.
about.conceito_body: The ENADE Concept is a 1-5 rating assigned to each course. The problem is that this integer hides the actual continuous score. Each band covers a range of roughly 1 point (for example, Concept 4 spans from 2.945 to 3.944). This means two courses with completely different scores, like 2.95 and 3.94, appear with the same Concept 4 in MEC's rankings. For the student, the consequence is direct: they may choose a university thinking it's at the same level as another, when the actual quality gap between them is enormous. In Medicine 2023, for example, 33% of courses with Concept 4 scored below 3.2, while others in the same band exceeded 3.7. Without access to the continuous score, this information is invisible.
about.origin_body: OpenEnade was born as RUE (Ranking Universitario Enade), a final project for the Software Engineering course in the Computer Science program at the Federal University of Campina Grande (UFCG), in Paraiba, Brazil. The idea was simple but powerful: expose the continuous ENADE score that MEC calculates but hides behind integer concepts.
about.what_happened_body: The original project used Go + Solr + React 16, hosted on Heroku. It ran until November 2022, when Heroku discontinued its free tier. The last commit was in April 2021. The original repositories remain public on GitHub as historical record.
about.resurrection_body: In April 2026, Emanuel Joivo (@joivo), one of the original team members, resurrected the project with an entirely new architecture: Python/Polars ETL, React 19 + Vite frontend, no backend (pre-processed static data). The motivation remains the same: bring real transparency to ENADE data for students choosing where to study.
about.team_body: OpenEnade is the result of work by alumni from the Computer Science program at UFCG.
about.tech_body: ETL: Python 3.12+ with Polars for processing INEP XLSX files. Frontend: React 19, Vite 7, TypeScript 5.9, Tailwind 4. Custom design system (@joivo/design-system). No backend: JSON data is generated by the ETL and served as static files. Hosted on Cloudflare Pages.
about.data_source_body: All data is public, obtained directly from the INEP/MEC portal (download.inep.gov.br). OpenEnade does not alter data. It only processes, organizes, and exposes the continuous score that INEP already calculates but does not highlight.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.what_is_enade_body: The National Student Performance Exam (ENADE) is administered by INEP/MEC to evaluate graduating students in undergraduate programs. The exam measures performance in relation to course content, competencies, and skills required for professional development.
methodology.cycle_body: ENADE evaluates different course groups each year in a 3-year cycle. This means a given course (e.g., Medicine) is evaluated every 3 years, and results can only be compared between institutions that took the same exam in the same year.
methodology.score_body: INEP calculates a continuous score from 0 to 5 for each course. This score is then converted into an integer "ENADE Concept" from 1 to 5. The problem: courses with very different scores can receive the same concept. A course scoring 2.95 and another scoring 3.94 both receive Concept 4.
methodology.comparison_body: Comparisons on OpenEnade are always between institutions that took the same exam in the same year. This ensures fairness: same test, same grading criteria, same student cohort.
methodology.caveat_body: The continuous score is standardized against the cohort's performance in each year. A score of 3.5 in 2019 and 3.5 in 2023 do not mean exactly the same thing, as the exams and standardization differ. The evolution chart shows trends, not directly comparable absolute values.
methodology.pipeline_body: We use an ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipeline, not ELT. This is intentional: INEP data arrives in XLSX files with inconsistencies across years (different column names, mixed decimal separators, varying sheet names). We need to normalize and validate before serving, not after. Loading dirty data into a database and transforming later would be risky for a project that needs reliable data from the first access.
methodology.pipeline_step_1: Ingestion: read INEP XLSX files, auto-detect the data sheet (the name changes between years)
methodology.pipeline_step_2: Transform: normalize columns (strip asterisks, spaces, accents), fix decimal separators (comma to period in 2022 dataset)
methodology.pipeline_step_3: Normalize: map network type (Public Federal/State/Municipal to PUBLIC, others to PRIVATE), modality, text standardization, remove SC (No Concept) records
methodology.pipeline_step_4: Ranking: compute national position within each (year, course) group, ordered by continuous score, with tiebreakers on CE and GE scores
methodology.pipeline_step_5: Export: generate one JSON per course/year, plus a general index (courses.json) for search
methodology.data_extracted_title: What we extract from each record
methodology.data_extracted_body: From each row of the INEP XLSX, we extract: year, course, institution abbreviation and name, state, city, network (public/private), modality (in-person/distance), number of graduating participants, general education raw score (GE), specific component raw score (SC), ENADE continuous score, and concept (band). No personal student data is processed. All data is aggregated by institution.
methodology.datasets_body: All files below are public and provided by INEP/MEC:
methodology.data_source_body: All data is public, obtained directly from the INEP/MEC portal (download.inep.gov.br). OpenEnade does not alter data. It only processes, organizes, and exposes the continuous score that INEP already calculates but does not highlight.
